scenes.text: How does a company go from two people in a room, to half a billion dollars? Let's follow one.
Day one. Maya and Alex start a company. They split it sixty forty. Ten million shares on paper, worth nothing yet.
Two words you need. Pre-money is what the company is worth before investors put cash in. Add the cash, and you get post-money. That's it.
Pre-seed round. Three angels chip in one million dollars. The company is worth four million afterward, so they own a quarter. Maya and Alex own less now. That's called dilution.
Seed round. Four million in, at twenty million post. Maya's slice shrinks to thirty-six percent. But each share is now worth four times what it was.
Series A. Ten million at fifty. The pie is the same shape, it's just worth a lot more.
Series B. Thirty million in. The company is worth a hundred and fifty million. Maya owns less than a quarter, and that quarter is worth thirty-four million dollars.
Look what happens. Her percentage goes down every round. Her dollars go up every round. That is the trade.
Five years later, Brewly sells for half a billion dollars. The angel who put in one million walks away with sixty-four. Maya, a hundred and fifteen.
A smaller slice of a much bigger pie. That's the whole game.
